title: Text Analysis: Obama Tweets
text:
In May 2015, Barack Obama became the first U.S. president with an official Twitter account. Over the following years, the @POTUS and @FLOTUS accounts documented the Obamas’ direct communication with the public. This study analyzes 4,339 tweets from both accounts to compare how Barack and Michelle Obama discuss three global issue areas: sports, climate, and diplomacy.
Specifically, I address three questions:

How do the two individuals differ in their attention to each issue area?
What are the emotional tones of their tweets across these three categories?
Which words best characterize each issue area, and how do their linguistic styles differ?

title: Text Analysis: Obama Tweets
text:
In May 2015, Barack Obama became the first U.S. president with an official Twitter account. Over the following years, the @POTUS and @FLOTUS accounts documented the Obamas’ direct communication with the public. This study analyzes 4,339 tweets from both accounts to compare how Barack and Michelle Obama discuss three global issue areas: sports, climate, and diplomacy.
Specifically, I address three questions:

How do the two individuals differ in their attention to each issue area?
What are the emotional tones of their tweets across these three categories?
Which words best characterize each issue area, and how do their linguistic styles differ?

title: Text Analysis: Obama Tweets
text:
Data and Methods

Data Loading


Keyword Definitions

sports_keywords <- "Olympics|Team USA|World Cup|athletes|game|team|players|sport|refugees|basketball|football|win|champion"
climate_keywords <- "climate|Paris|agreement|clean|environment|energy|global warming|emissions|green|sustainable|earth"
diplomacy_keywords <- "visit|Cuba|Kenya|Laos|Iran|deal|historic|partnership|foreign|ambassador|meeting|talks|allies|nations"



Text Categorization


Results
Issue Distribution

tweets_cat |>
  count(person, category) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = category, values_from = n, values_fill = 0)

# A tibble: 2 × 4
  person   climate diplomacy sports
  <chr>      <int>     <int>  <int>
1 Barack        22        19     24
2 Michelle      15       121    161


The dataset contains 321 tweets from Barack Obama and 4,018 from Michelle Obama. After filtering, Michelle has 161 sports-related tweets and 121 diplomacy-related tweets, but only 15 on climate. Barack’s relevant tweets are evenly distributed across all three categories (sports 24, diplomacy 19, climate 22). This reflects their differing public roles.


High-Frequency Words


Joining with `by = join_by(word)`









Figure 1 shows the top 10 high-frequency words for Barack and Michelle across the three categories. On climate, Barack’s top words include “climate,” “change,” and “agreement.” Michelle has no climate words in her top 20, reflecting her limited climate coverage. On diplomacy, Barack emphasizes “iran” and “deal,” while Michelle uses “visit” and “partnership.” On sports, Michelle’s vocabulary is more diverse, including “refugees” and “athletes.”


Sentiment Analysis


Joining with `by = join_by(word)`









Both Obamas maintain predominantly positive tones. Michelle’s sports coverage is exceptionally positive (96.4% positive), while Barack’s sports coverage shows more balance (74.4% positive, 25.6% negative).


TF-IDF Analysis









The TF-IDF analysis identifies words uniquely characteristic of each category. For climate: “climate,” “agreement,” “change.” For diplomacy: “visit,” “partnership,” “meeting.” For sports: “team,” “winners,” “athletes,” and notably “refugees.”

title: Text Analysis: Obama Tweets
text:
Conclusion
Three main findings:

Differential focus: Michelle emphasizes sports and diplomacy; Barack distributes attention evenly across all three categories.
Consistently positive messaging: Both maintain positive tones, but Michelle’s sports coverage is exceptionally positive (96.4%).
Distinctive vocabularies: Barack uses policy-focused language; Michelle uses broader relational and humanitarian terms.

title: US State-Level Data Maps
text:
This page presents choropleth maps using data from the poliscidata package. I chose two variables: carfatal07 (numeric) and cook_index3 (categorical).
Data source: poliscidata::states

title: US State-Level Data Maps
text:
Setup & Data Preparation

# Load libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(maps)
library(sf)
library(leaflet)
library(viridis)
library(poliscidata)

# 1. Get geographic data for static maps (maps package)
states_polygon <- as_tibble(map_data("state")) |>
  select(region, group, order, lat, long)

# 2. Get geographic data for interactive maps (sf package)
states_sf <- read_sf("https://rstudio.github.io/leaflet/json/us-states.geojson") |>
  select(name, geometry)

# 3. Load poliscidata and prepare variables
polisci_data <- as_tibble(poliscidata::states) |>
  select(state, carfatal07, cook_index3)

# Clean keys for merging: lower case and remove spaces
polisci_data <- polisci_data |>
  mutate(state = str_to_lower(str_squish(state)))

states_polygon <- states_polygon |>
  mutate(region = str_to_lower(region))

states_sf <- states_sf |>
  mutate(name = str_to_lower(name))

# Merge for static plot
static_data <- states_polygon |>
  left_join(polisci_data, by = c("region" = "state")) |>
  filter(!region %in% c("alaska", "hawaii"))

# Merge for interactive plot
sf_data <- states_sf |>
  left_join(polisci_data, by = c("name" = "state")) |>
  filter(!name %in% c("alaska", "hawaii", "puerto rico")) |>
  mutate(geometry = st_make_valid(geometry))


Numeric Variable: Traffic Fatalities (carfatal07)
Car crash fatalities per 100,000 people in 2007. #### Static Map (ggplot2)

static_data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group)) + 
  geom_polygon(aes(fill = carfatal07), color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) + 
  coord_map() + 
  theme_void() + 
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Fatalities\n(per 100k)",
                       option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Vehicle Crash Fatalities by State (2007)")









Interactive Map (leaflet)

# Create color palette
pal_num <- colorNumeric("viridis", domain = sf_data$carfatal07)

# Create interactive labels
sf_data <- sf_data |>
  mutate(labels_num = str_c(str_to_title(name), ": ", 
                            round(carfatal07, 1), " per 100k"))

leaflet(sf_data) |>
  addTiles() |>
  setView(-96, 37.8, 4) |>
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal_num(carfatal07),
    weight = 1,
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    color = "white",
    label = ~labels_num,
    labelOptions = labelOptions(textsize = "12px"),
    highlightOptions = highlightOptions(weight = 3, color = "#666")
  )







Insights
The numeric map reveals significant regional variation. Southern states like Mississippi and West Virginia show the highest fatality rates, while states in the Northeast generally have lower rates. This pattern may reflect differences in rural versus urban driving conditions, speed limits, and emergency response capabilities.



Categorical Variable: Cook Partisan Voting Index (cook_index3)
Cook Political Report partisan lean: “D+8+”, “Competitive”, or “R+8+”.

Static Map (ggplot2)

static_data |>
  mutate(cook_cat = factor(cook_index3, 
                          levels = c("D+8+", "Competitive", "R+8+"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group)) + 
  geom_polygon(aes(fill = cook_cat), color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) + 
  coord_map() + 
  theme_void() + 
  scale_fill_manual(name = "Partisan Lean",
                    values = c("D+8+" = "blue", 
                               "Competitive" = "purple", 
                               "R+8+" = "red"),
                    na.value = "grey90") +
  labs(title = "Cook Partisan Voting Index by State")










Interactive Map (leaflet)

# Prepare data for interactive map
sf_data <- sf_data |>
  mutate(cook_cat = factor(cook_index3, 
                          levels = c("D+8+", "Competitive", "R+8+")))

# Create color palette for categorical variable
pal_cat <- colorFactor(c("blue", "purple", "red"), 
                       domain = sf_data$cook_cat,
                       na.color = "grey90")

# Create labels
sf_data <- sf_data |>
  mutate(labels_cat = str_c(str_to_title(name), ": ", cook_index3))

leaflet(sf_data) |>
  addTiles() |>
  setView(-96, 37.8, 4) |>
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal_cat(cook_cat),
    weight = 1,
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    color = "white",
    label = ~labels_cat,
    labelOptions = labelOptions(textsize = "12px"),
    highlightOptions = highlightOptions(weight = 3, color = "#666")
  ) |>
  addLegend(pal = pal_cat, values = ~cook_cat, opacity = 0.7,
            title = "Partisan Lean", position = "bottomright")







Insights
The categorical map shows a clear geographic pattern of partisanship. Democratic-leaning states (blue) cluster in the Northeast and West Coast, while Republican-leaning states (red) dominate the South and Midwest. Competitive states (purple) are scattered, often around the Great Lakes region.

title: US State-Level Data Maps
text:
Summary
This page demonstrates four choropleth maps (static + interactive for both numeric and categorical variables) using real US state data from the poliscidata package. Interactive maps allow users to hover over each state to see exact values.
